content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 23, 2016

Thomas T. Watson
City Attorney
1901 Airport Road, Suite 300
South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-176

Dear Mr. Watson:

This letter responds to your request on behalf of Councilmember Tom Davis, a member of
the City Council for the City of South Lake Tahoe, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Under the Act, may Councilmember Davis take part in a City Council decision to uphold or
reverse the denial of a permit for use of a home as a Vacation Home Rental ("VHR") property
where he owns a 10% interest in a company (the "Company") that manages VHR properties located
in the city?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
Company or Councilmember Davis.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of South Lake Tahoe and are requesting advice on
behalf of Councilmember Davis. The City of South Lake Tahoe issues permits for the use of
residential properties as VHR properties. The City Council will be hearing an appeal of the
Planning Commission's denial of an application for a permit.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-176
Page No. 2

The Company manages approximately 175 vacation home rentals, (fewer than ten percent), of the total of 1,800 such properties in the city. There are over ten VHR companies or local agencies handling the bulk of the VHR business although some owners manage their own properties. If the Company gets a contract to manage this property, the Company's revenues would increase by less than 1.5%.

The Company does not currently, nor has it during the previous 12 months, managed the property that is the subject of the appeal. Also, the Company does not and has not during the previous 12 months managed other properties or otherwise received income from the applicant.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official form making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. They include the following:

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.)

Councilmember has an interest in the Company as a 10% owner and as a source of income to him.²

Foreseeability

Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Councilmember Davis's business is not explicitly involved in the decisions regarding the appeal because it is not a named party in or the subject of the decision. When an interest is not explicitly involved in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

² If a public official owns a 10-percent or greater interest in a business, customers who are sources of income to that business are also considered sources of income to the official if income derived from a particular client or customer aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the government decision. (Section 87103(c).) You state that Councilmember Davis's company does not currently, nor has it during the previous 12 months, received any income from the applicant. Therefore, the applicant is not a source of income to Councilmember Davis.

File No. A-16-176
Page No. 3

One of the factors considered in determining whether a financial effect is reasonably
foreseeable is "[W]hether the governmental decision will provide or deny an opportunity, or create
an advantage or disadvantage for one of the official's financial interests, including whether the
financial interest may be entitled to compete or be eligible for a benefit resulting from the decision."
(Regulation 18701(b)(5).). You state that the Company is one of ten VHR companies or local
agencies handling the bulk of the city's VHR property management. A decision to add another
property to the VHR property inventory would provide a business opportunity to this relatively
small number of property managers, including the Company. Under these facts, a financial effect on
the Company is a realistic possibility and, therefore, reasonably foreseeable.

Materiality

Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards for a public official's financial
interest in a business entity. Under Regulation 18702.1(a), an effect is material if a prudent person
with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a
change in the value of the business entity. The regulation also provides examples of decisions that
may have a material effect on a business entity, including a decision that would increase or decrease
the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies. (Regulation 18702.1(b)(3).)

You state that there are approximately 1,800 VHR properties in the city and the Company
manages fewer than 10% of these. You also state that if the Company gets a contract to manage this
property, the Company's revenues would increase by less than 1.5%. It is unlikely that such a small
increase in a business entity's revenues would materially affect its value. Therefore, a decision
whether to uphold or reverse the denial of the permit will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Councilmember Davis' property. This advice assumes Councilmember
Davis' is not reviewing numerous permits for the use of homes as VHR properties.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl
